title: Newsfeed Anger and Information Hygiene
dek: *The endless scroll of outrage, hot takes, and manufactured conflict doesn't just steal your time - it rewires your nervous system's baseline. Information hygiene is the practice of treating your attention as sacred ground, cultivating discernment about what crosses the threshold of your awareness, and learning to metabolize anger as fuel for change rather than letting it ferment into chronic stress.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Your newsfeed is engineered to trigger anger, and most of us scroll through it without any filter for what we let into our nervous system. Information hygiene treats your attention as a living boundary that needs daily maintenance, not just occasional crisis management. When you learn to choose inputs with the same care you bring to food or sleep, anger becomes useful signal instead of background static.

You have been told that staying informed means staying plugged in. But somewhere between the third doomscroll of the morning and the fifth argument with a stranger in a comment thread, the line between awareness and saturation blurs. Your chest tightens. Your jaw sets. You feel the familiar heat rising, that low-grade fury that has become the emotional weather of being online. In our experience, most people don't realize they're angry until the anger has already colonized their body. It shows up as tension between the shoulder blades, a clenched stomach, the grinding of teeth during a Zoom call. The newsfeed delivers its payload in micro-doses throughout the day, each headline calibrated to spike your nervous system just enough to keep you scrolling. This is not an accident. Platforms profit from engagement, and anger is one of the most reliable engagement drivers we have. A 2013 study published in the *Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences* found that content expressing moral outrage spreads faster and further than any other type of emotional content. The algorithm learns what makes you click, and it serves you more of it. ## The Architecture of Manufactured Outrage Newsfeed design exploits a feature of human cognition that once kept us alive: the negativity bias. Our ancestors survived by paying more attention to threats than to opportunities. A rustling in the grass could be wind or it could be a predator, and the ones who assumed predator lived to pass on their genes. But the newsfeed is not the savanna. The threats it presents are often abstract, distant, or entirely fabricated. Yet your nervous system responds as if each piece of outrage bait were a clear and present danger. Your amygdala lights up. Cortisol floods your bloodstream. Your body prepares for a fight that never comes. Beverly Engel, in her work on emotional abuse and anger, points out that chronic exposure to triggering content without resolution creates a state of perpetual activation. You are angry, but there is no immediate action to take, no way to discharge the energy. So it accumulates. It becomes the background hum of your inner life, a constant low-level irritation that colors everything else. The platforms know this. They have entire teams dedicated to improving for what they call "time on site." Every feature - the infinite scroll, the notification badge, the autoplay video - is designed to keep you in a state of heightened arousal just shy of actual discomfort. You are not the customer. You are the product being sold to advertisers. ## What Information Hygiene Actually Means Information hygiene is not about ignorance or avoidance. It is about discernment. It is the practice of treating your attention as a finite, sacred resource and making conscious choices about where you direct it. Think of it this way: you would not eat food without considering its source, its freshness, its nutritional value. You would not invite a stranger into your home without asking who they are and why they are there. Yet most of us scroll through our feeds with zero filter, letting any content that has been algorithmically selected for maximum emotional impact flow directly into our awareness. Information hygiene begins with recognizing that not all information is created equal. Some information nourishes. It deepens your understanding, expands your compassion, connects you to something larger than your immediate circumstances. Other information depletes. It triggers outrage without offering any path to meaningful action, or it presents problems so vast and abstract that engaging with them only generates helplessness. The practice is not about creating an echo chamber or retreating into willful ignorance. It is about choosing your inputs with intention. It is about asking, before you click, before you scroll, before you engage: *Does this serve my capacity to respond to the world with clarity and compassion, or does it simply activate my nervous system for someone else's profit?* Over the years I've seen clients who describe themselves as "news junkies" or "politically engaged" who are, in fact, caught in a cycle of compulsive consumption. They check their feeds dozens of times a day. They read every update, every hot take, every thread. And they feel worse - more anxious, more angry, more powerless - with each passing week. ## The Body Keeps the Score (of Every Scroll) Your body does not distinguish between a physical threat and a perceived one. When you read a headline designed to enrage you, your heart rate increases. Your blood pressure rises. Your muscles tense. This is PubMed research on anger expression and cardiovascular load made visible in real time. If this happens once or twice, your body recovers easily. But when it happens dozens of times a day, every day, for months or years, the effects compound. You develop what clinicians call allostatic load - the cumulative wear and tear on your body from chronic stress. Your baseline shifts. You become quicker to anger, slower to calm. The threshold for activation lowers. I have felt this in my own body. The tight band across my forehead after too much time on Twitter. The nausea that comes from scrolling through disaster footage. The way my hands shake slightly when I close the laptop after a deep dive into some fresh atrocity. These are not abstract symptoms. They are my nervous system telling me that I have exceeded my capacity to metabolize what I am taking in. Soraya Chemaly, in her exploration of women's anger, emphasizes that anger itself is not the problem. Anger is information. It tells you that something is wrong, that a boundary has been violated, that change is needed. But when anger becomes chronic background noise, it loses its signal quality. It becomes static. You stop being able to distinguish between the anger that points toward meaningful action and the anger that is simply a conditioned response to engineered triggers. The body knows the difference. Useful anger feels clear and directed. It has heat but also focus. It moves through you and asks to be translated into action. Manufactured anger feels murky and diffuse. It churns without resolution. It leaves you exhausted and empty. ## Building Your Information Diet Start by taking inventory. For three days, track what you consume. Not just the major news sites or the articles you read all the way through, but every scroll, every glance at a notification, every headline that catches your eye as you move through your digital day. Notice what happens in your body as you consume. Does your breathing change? Does your jaw clench? Do you feel energized or depleted? Curious or enraged? Connected or isolated? Then ask: What percentage of this is actually helping me engage with the world in a way that aligns with my values? What percentage is just activating my nervous system for no productive purpose? From there, you can begin to choose. This might mean unfollowing accounts that consistently spike your nervous system without offering insight or connection. It might mean setting specific times for checking news rather than grazing all day. It might mean choosing one or two trusted sources and letting the rest go. Some people benefit from what we describe across the articles we've published on this site as "media fasting" - taking a full day or week away from all news consumption. The first few hours are often uncomfortable. You feel the pull of habit, the fear of missing something important. But by the second day, something shifts. Your nervous system begins to settle. The background hum of low-grade outrage starts to quiet. When you return to your newsfeed after a fast, you often see it differently. The manufactured quality of the outrage becomes more visible. The addictive pull of the scroll loses some of its grip. You have created space to choose rather than simply react. ## A Five-Minute Practice for Grounding Before You Scroll Before you open your newsfeed - before you check the news, before you scroll through social media, before you dive into the comment threads - try this: Sit for a moment with your feet flat on the floor. Take three slow breaths, each one deeper than the last. Notice the weight of your body in the chair, the temperature of the air on your skin, the sounds in the room around you. Ask yourself: What is my intention in consuming this information right now? Am I seeking connection, understanding, a specific piece of news I need for a decision I am making? Or am I just filling time, distracting myself from something uncomfortable, following the pull of habit? If you have a clear intention, proceed. If you do not, consider doing something else. Go for a walk. Call a friend. Read a chapter of a book. Make yourself a cup of tea - perhaps [Traditional Medicinals Throat Coat (16 bags)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00B77XTA8?tag=spankyspinola-20) if your throat feels tight from all the words you have swallowed. This small pause creates a circuit breaker. It interrupts the automatic reach for the phone, the compulsive refresh. It gives you a moment to choose. ## The Metabolic Capacity for Anger Thomas Moore, in his work on the soul's relationship to difficult emotions, suggests that we each have a particular capacity for metabolizing psychic material. Some of us can process a great deal of challenging content and remain grounded. Others become quickly overwhelmed. There is no moral value attached to this capacity. It is simply a fact of your particular nervous system, shaped by your history, your temperament, your current resources. Recognizing your own capacity is an act of self-knowledge, not self-judgment. If you find that you are constantly overwhelmed, constantly angry, constantly activated by what you consume, that is not a sign that you are weak or ignorant or insufficiently committed to justice. It is a sign that you are taking in more than you can metabolize. The solution is not to consume more in an attempt to toughen up. It is to right-size your input to match your current capacity. This might mean limiting your news consumption to 20 minutes a day. It might mean choosing text over video, because reading allows you to control the pace of intake in a way that video does not. It might mean focusing your attention on local issues where you can actually take meaningful action, rather than global crises that leave you feeling helpless. Your capacity is not fixed. It can expand over time as you develop practices for processing and discharging anger in healthy ways. The [fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit) on this site offers specific techniques for working with anger as energy rather than letting it accumulate as tension. But expansion requires that you work within your current capacity, not beyond it. ## When Anger Becomes Action The point of information hygiene is not to insulate yourself from the world's suffering or to retreat into comfortable ignorance. It is to preserve your capacity to respond. When you are constantly activated, constantly outraged, constantly overwhelmed, you become less effective, not more. You burn out. You become cynical. You lose the ability to distinguish between problems you can actually address and problems that are simply being presented to you for engagement farming. But when you choose your inputs with care, when you protect your nervous system from chronic activation, when you give yourself space to process what you take in, you create the conditions for meaningful response. Your anger becomes clarified. It points toward specific actions rather than diffuse rage. You might discover that you have energy to volunteer for a local organization addressing an issue you care about. You might find that you can have a difficult conversation with a family member without losing your center. You might realize that you can write a letter, make a donation, show up to a meeting - small, concrete actions that actually move the needle rather than just discharging your nervous system's need to do something, anything, in response to the latest outrage. This is what we mean when we talk about anger as fuel. Not the chronic, depleting anger of the newsfeed scroll, but the clarified, directed anger that arises from clear seeing and moves toward purposeful action. The kind of anger that changes things. ## The Social Pressure to Stay Plugged In One of the challenges of practicing information hygiene is the social pressure to stay constantly informed. There is a performative quality to outrage culture, a sense that if you are not visibly angry about the latest atrocity, you are complicit in it. This is a false choice. You can care deeply about justice, about the suffering of others, about the state of the world, without subjecting yourself to a constant stream of triggering content. In fact, you can probably care more effectively when you are not chronically activated. But naming this to others can be difficult. When someone asks if you have seen the latest news and you say no, there is often a moment of judgment. An assumption that you do not care, that you are privileged enough to tune out, that you are part of the problem. It helps to have language ready. "I am being intentional about my media consumption right now" is a complete sentence. So is "I am focusing my attention on issues where I can take meaningful action." You do not owe anyone an explanation of your information diet any more than you owe them an explanation of what you eat for breakfast. For some, it helps to build community around these practices. Finding others who are also working to cultivate information hygiene can provide support and accountability. The community at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) offers space for these conversations, as does [the about page](/about) here, which outlines the values behind this work. ## Cleaning Your Feeds Like You Clean Your Body Think of information hygiene as parallel to physical hygiene. You shower regularly not because you are dirty in some moral sense, but because your body accumulates grime through normal interaction with the world. You brush your teeth not once when they are already decayed, but daily as preventive care. Information hygiene works the same way. You do not wait until you are completely overwhelmed and burned out to take a break from the newsfeed. You build regular practices of clearing and cleansing into your daily routine. This might look like a weekly review of who you follow, unfollowing accounts that consistently trigger you without offering value. It might look like a monthly audit of your app usage, deleting the ones that have become compulsive time sinks. It might look like a nightly practice of closing all browsers and putting your phone in another room an hour before bed. Some people find it helpful to think of their digital environment as a physical space that needs regular cleaning. Just as you would not let trash pile up in your kitchen, you do not let toxic content accumulate in your feeds. Just as you might burn sage or open windows to clear stale energy from a room - perhaps after a cleansing shower with [Banyan Botanicals Neem & Aloe Soap - 4 oz Bar](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B009TE7H5O?tag=spankyspinola-20) - you regularly clear your digital spaces of what no longer serves. The [assessments hub](/assessments) on this site includes tools for evaluating your relationship to digital media and identifying patterns that might need attention. These are not one-time exercises but ongoing practices, revisited as your circumstances and capacity change. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### Is not reading the news a form of privilege? The question assumes that consuming large volumes of distressing news is the only way to stay informed or to care about justice. This is not true. You can be deeply engaged with the world's suffering and still choose to choose your inputs carefully. In fact, the people who sustain long-term commitment to social change are often those who have learned to protect their nervous systems from chronic activation. Privilege is not about how much news you consume. It is about the material conditions of your life and how you use whatever resources you have - including your attention and your energy - in service of change. ### How do I know the difference between healthy information hygiene and avoidance? Avoidance is driven by fear and results in constriction. You avoid information because you cannot tolerate the discomfort it brings, and over time your world becomes smaller. Information hygiene is driven by discernment and results in expansion. You choose your inputs so that you have the capacity to engage deeply with what matters most, and over time your ability to respond to the world grows. The felt sense is different. Avoidance feels like hiding. Hygiene feels like tending. ### What if something important happens and I miss it? If something is truly important and relevant to your life, you will hear about it. You do not need to monitor the newsfeed constantly to stay informed about what actually matters. The fear of missing out is real, but it is also largely manufactured by platforms that profit from your constant attention. Trust that the truly significant news will reach you through other channels - conversations with friends, community meetings, the sources you have chosen to follow. Everything else is noise. ## Tending the Garden of Attention There is a practice in Ayurveda of taking [Banyan Botanicals Organic Shatavari Tablets - 90 Tablets](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000703AQ4?tag=spankyspinola-20) to support the body's natural resilience to stress. The principle is not to eliminate stress but to strengthen the system's capacity to meet it without becoming depleted. Information hygiene works on a similar principle. You are not trying to eliminate all exposure to difficult content. You are building your capacity to engage with the world's pain and complexity without losing yourself in the process. This is slow work. It goes against the grain of a culture that valorizes constant connectivity and treats attention as an infinite resource to be exploited. It requires that you swim upstream, that you set boundaries that others might not understand, that you trust your own nervous system's signals over the algorithmic suggestions of what you should care about right now. But the fruit of this work is a life where anger can be what it was meant to be: a clarifying force, a signal of values violated, a call to purposeful action. Not the chronic background static of manufactured outrage, but the clean heat of righteous response. The kind of anger that does not just consume you but that you can actually use to change the world. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 28, 2026. In our work on newsfeed anger and information hygiene, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
